title: Building Effective AI Coding Agents for the Terminal: Scaffolding, Harness, Context Engineering, and Lessons Learned
storage_format: ACE Playbook (bullets of learned strategies, scored by effectiveness + recency + semantic scoring, selected for injection into the system prompt) + Session Storage (full JSON transcripts with metadata, persisted per session for continuation) + Snapshot Manager (tracking of file operations via git for per-step undo).
llm_used: Model-agnostic (supports Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Alibaba, etc. via per-workflow model binding with 5 model roles: action, thinking, critique, vision, compact)
benchmarks_used: NR (mentions Terminal-Bench and LongCLI-Bench but does not formally evaluate)
baseline_compared: NR (qualitatively compares with Claude Code, Goose, OpenCode, Crush, Aider, SWE-Agent, OpenHands)
memory_size_over_time: Playbook grows with learnings; context compaction in 5 progressive stages (70%→99% utilization); prompt caching reduces input token cost by ~88%
token_reduction: ~88% input token reduction via prompt caching (Anthropic); context compaction in 5 stages (warning→masking→pruning→aggressive masking→full LLM summarization)
main_result: Technical report describing OpenDev, an open-source terminal-native agent in Rust with the ACE memory system (Playbook + Sessions), adaptive context compaction in 5 stages, 5 defense-in-depth security layers, and a multi-model architecture with per-workflow binding. No formal empirical evaluation.
limitations: No empirical evaluation on benchmarks; no quantitative comparison with other agents; the paper is a descriptive technical report; effectiveness of the ACE memory system not experimentally validated; single author
validity_threats: Lack of empirical evaluation makes claims unverifiable; comparisons with proprietary systems (Claude Code) based on external observation; design decisions justified by development experience but without ablations; generalization to different workflows untested
